prerequisites.ufw_missing: UFW is not installed — install it with: sudo apt install ufw
prerequisites.ss_missing: ss not available — install iproute2: sudo apt install iproute2

firewall.inactive: UFW firewall is disabled. Your machine is currently unprotected.
firewall.policy_open: Default incoming policy is set to ALLOW — all ports are open by default
firewall.policy_unknown: Could not determine default incoming policy

rules.no_open_any: No 'allow from any' rule without port restriction detected
rules.open_any_found: Rule allowing all incoming connections without port restriction: {rule}
rules.ipv6_ok: IPv6 configuration consistent with UFW rules
rules.ipv6_missing: UFW rules exist for IPv4 but no equivalent IPv6 rules found

state.active_enabled: Service active and set to start automatically at boot
state.active_disabled: Service is currently active but will not restart automatically
state.inactive_enabled: Service is stopped but enabled at boot — may start at next reboot
state.inactive_disabled: {label} is installed but stopped and disabled. No immediate risk.

ports.all_covered: All ports listening on 0.0.0.0 are covered by a UFW rule
ports.uncovered: Port {port} listening on all interfaces without explicit UFW rule
ports.ephemeral_ignored: Ephemeral port ignored (>{threshold}) — kernel-assigned temporary port: {port}
ports.system_port: Internal system port — no risk (legitimate OS service, no UFW rule needed): {port} ({service})
ports.listening_count: {count} listening port(s) detected on this system

logs.no_logfile: UFW log file not found (/var/log/ufw.log) — logging disabled?
logs.empty: No UFW BLOCK events found in logs for the analysed period
logs.brute_none: No bruteforce detected in the analysed period
logs.svc_hits_none: No attempts on installed service ports
logs.geo_unavailable: whois not available — install it for IP geolocation (sudo apt install whois)
logs.geoip2_unavailable: GeoIP2 not available — install python3-geoip2 for IP geolocation
logs.geoip2_no_db: GeoIP2 installed but no GeoLite2 database found — install it with: sudo apt install geoip-database

ddns.none: No DDNS client detected — no DDNS-based external exposure identified
ddns.no_open_ports: No UFW port open without source restriction detected
ddns.ports_title: Ports potentially exposed to the internet
ddns.warn: DDNS active with open port(s) without source restriction — verify exposure is intentional
ddns.high_warn: High-risk service potentially internet-exposed via DDNS
ddns.advice: If this exposure is intentional: keep services up to date, enable authentication, and consider Fail2ban.
ddns.inactive: DDNS client installed but service is inactive

docker.iptables_disabled: Docker configured with iptables disabled (daemon.json). UFW rules apply normally.
docker.iptables_bypass: Docker may bypass UFW rules — iptables is not disabled in daemon.json
docker.no_containers: No running Docker containers with exposed ports
docker.iptables_bypass_detail1: This is an architectural choice, not a simple fix — three options:
docker.iptables_bypass_detail2: 1. Disable Docker iptables (advanced): set {"iptables": false} in daemon.json — you manage networking yourself
docker.iptables_bypass_detail3: 2. Add explicit UFW rules for your container ports (safer, recommended)

summary.clean: Your configuration is sound. No action required.
summary.warnings: Warnings reflect a normal configuration for this type of system. No immediate action required.
summary.alerts: Corrections are needed. Address items marked 'Action required' first.
summary.implicit_policy: Service(s) relying on default UFW policy rather than explicit rules. Correct if policy is deny, but an explicit rule is more robust.
summary.scope_line1: This score reflects your UFW firewall configuration only.
summary.scope_line2: System updates, application security, and other attack vectors are outside the scope of this audit.

fixes.title: AVAILABLE FIXES
fixes.done: Fixes complete. Re-run the audit to verify.

report.title: UFW-AUDIT REPORT
report.next_2: Review warnings and assess their impact.
report.next_3: Re-run the audit after each change to verify.

cli.help_verbose: -v, --verbose           Show detailed port exposure for each service
cli.help_detailed: -d, --detailed          Save full audit report to a log file
cli.help_fix: --fix                   Offer to apply automatic corrections after the audit
cli.help_reconf: --reconfigure           Reset saved port configuration and re-ask
cli.help_jsonfull: --json-full             Export full audit details as JSON
cli.help_logdays: --log-days=N            Analyse the last N days of UFW logs (default: 7)
cli.help_default: Run without options for a standard audit with coloured output.

errors.must_be_root: This script must be run as root: sudo ufw-audit
errors.ufw_not_found: UFW is not installed on this system

ssh_server.exposure: Heavily targeted by automated scanners and brute-force attacks — internet-exposed by default
ssh_server.threat: Full shell access to the machine, privilege escalation, lateral movement to other network systems

vnc_server.exposure: Often lacks native encryption; weak or absent authentication on older versions
vnc_server.threat: Full visual and interactive desktop control — equivalent to physical access to the machine

ftp_server.exposure: Unencrypted protocol — credentials and data transmitted in plain text over the network
ftp_server.threat: Credential theft, file access and exfiltration, potential pivot if credentials are reused

postgresql.exposure: Password or configurable authentication — direct exposure is rarely intentional
postgresql.threat: Full database exfiltration, system command execution via extensions (pg_execute_server_program)

redis.exposure: No authentication by default historically, very frequently misconfigured and exposed
redis.threat: Read/write access to all data, remote code execution (RCE) via configuration — documented and actively exploited attack vector

wireguard_vpn.exposure: Intentionally internet-facing for VPN function — limited attack surface by design
wireguard_vpn.threat: Full internal network access if tunnel is compromised or private keys are stolen

home_assistant.exposure: Web interface controlling physical devices — built-in authentication but often exposed for remote access
home_assistant.threat: Control of physical devices (locks, alarms, switches), local network access via automations, home automation data exfiltration

nextcloud.exposure: Personal file server exposed to the internet — authentication required but significant attack surface depending on installed apps
nextcloud.threat: Access to all personal files, contacts and calendars stored, potential pivot if credentials are compromised

samba_windows_file_sharing.exposure: Designed exclusively for local networks — inherently dangerous if exposed to the internet
samba_windows_file_sharing.threat: File access and encryption (ransomware), lateral movement (EternalBlue/WannaCry), data exfiltration

mysql___mariadb.exposure: Password-based authentication, history of unauthenticated access CVEs
mysql___mariadb.threat: Full database exfiltration, data destruction, application-level access if credentials are shared

cockpit_web_admin.exposure: Web-based system administration interface — authentication required but significant attack surface
cockpit_web_admin.threat: Full system administration if compromised: service management, users, network, root terminal

mosquitto_mqtt.exposure: MQTT broker often without authentication by default, listens on all interfaces
mosquitto_mqtt.threat: Unauthorised publish/subscribe to home automation topics, device control, sensor data interception

virt.bypass_risk: {name} detected — {iface} bridge interface may insert iptables rules, bypassing UFW FORWARD policy
virt.snap_network: Snap package '{pkg}' has active network connections — verify it does not bypass UFW
